create-focused-commits.label: Create Focused Commits
create-focused-commits.summary: Record one coherent staged change with a concise message that makes the project timeline readable.

sketch0.title: A Commit Is a Deliberate Checkpoint

sketch2.title: Write the Message for a Reader

try_create_focused_commits_sketch0.title: Commit One Coherent Change
try_create_focused_commits_sketch0.prompt:
The navigation files are staged. Commit them with `Add events navigation`, then inspect the latest one-line entry.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_create_focused_commits_sketch0.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: A focused commit records one understandable idea with a message that explains the result.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_create_focused_commits_sketch0.starterCode:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.
try_create_focused_commits_sketch0.solutionCode:
cd community-site
git commit -m "Add events navigation"
git log -1 --oneline
try_create_focused_commits_sketch0.instructions:
The navigation files are staged. Commit them with `Add events navigation`, then inspect the latest one-line entry.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

main_sh.content:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home"];
' > nav.js
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s '<nav>Home Events</nav>
' > index.html
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const links = ["Home", "Events"];
' > nav.js
cd ..
git -C community-site add -- index.html nav.js

main_sh.content:
cd community-site
git commit -m "Add events navigation"
git log -1 --oneline

try_create_focused_commits_sketch1.title: Leave Unrelated Work for Later
try_create_focused_commits_sketch1.prompt:
Only `README.md` is staged; `styles.css` is still an unstaged experiment. Commit the documentation change as `Clarify volunteer hours` and verify that the style edit remains.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_create_focused_commits_sketch1.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: Committing staged work does not sweep unrelated unstaged edits into history.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_create_focused_commits_sketch1.starterCode:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.
try_create_focused_commits_sketch1.solutionCode:
cd community-site
git commit -m "Clarify volunteer hours"
git status --short
try_create_focused_commits_sketch1.instructions:
Only `README.md` is staged; `styles.css` is still an unstaged experiment. Commit the documentation change as `Clarify volunteer hours` and verify that the style edit remains.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s 'Open Saturdays.
' > README.md
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'body { color: navy; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s 'Open Saturdays from 9 AM to noon.
' > README.md
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'body { color: teal; }
' > styles.css
cd ..
git -C community-site add -- README.md

main_sh.content:
cd community-site
git commit -m "Clarify volunteer hours"
git status --short

try_create_focused_commits_sketch2.title: Use an Imperative Commit Message
try_create_focused_commits_sketch2.prompt:
A bug fix in `app.js` is staged. Commit it with `Fix event card toggle`, then print only the latest subject line.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_create_focused_commits_sketch2.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: Imperative messages describe what the snapshot does when applied to the project.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_create_focused_commits_sketch2.starterCode:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.
try_create_focused_commits_sketch2.solutionCode:
cd community-site
git commit -m "Fix event card toggle"
git log -1 --format=%s
try_create_focused_commits_sketch2.instructions:
A bug fix in `app.js` is staged. Commit it with `Fix event card toggle`, then print only the latest subject line.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

main_sh.content:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s 'const open = false;
' > app.js
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s 'const open = !open;
' > app.js
cd ..
git -C community-site add -- app.js

main_sh.content:
cd community-site
git commit -m "Fix event card toggle"
git log -1 --format=%s

help.concept: A sequence of focused commits reads like a concise explanation of how the project grew.

Community Hub

single-choice-1.title: Commit History Inspection
single-choice-1.prompt: Which command provides a concise view of the commit history?
single-choice-1.hint: Look for a command that summarizes commit messages.

help.concept: A concise history view helps quickly understand changes.
help.hint_2: It includes the `--oneline` option for brevity.

multi-choice-1.title: Purpose of Commits
multi-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of creating commits in Git?
multi-choice-1.hint: Think about how commits help in tracking changes.

help.concept: Commits are snapshots of your project at a point in time.
help.hint_1: Commits help in tracking changes and reverting to previous states.
help.hint_2: They are essential for collaboration and understanding project history.

multi-choice-2.title: Commit Message Best Practices
multi-choice-2.prompt: Which of the following are best practices for writing commit messages?
multi-choice-2.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: A focused commit records one coherent change with a message that explains the result.
help.hint_1: Compare the question to the lesson example and remove details that do not match the requested concept.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

drag-reorder-1.title: Commit Process Steps
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps to create a commit in the correct order.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Think about the sequence from making changes to committing them.

help.concept: Creating a commit involves staging changes and then committing them.
help.hint_2: You need to write a commit message after staging.

tokens.t1: Make changes in the working directory

fill-blank-1.title: Staging Command
fill-blank-1.prompt: Complete the command to stage a file named `app.js`.
fill-blank-1.hint: Use the `git add` command to stage files.

help.concept: Staging prepares files for a commit.
help.hint_2: You need to specify the file name after `git add`.

fill-blank-2.title: Commit Message Format
fill-blank-2.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the commit message: `git commit -m '[blank1]'`.
fill-blank-2.hint: Commit messages should be in imperative form.

help.concept: Commit messages describe the change's purpose.
help.hint_1: The message should be concise and imperative.
help.hint_2: Think about what the commit achieves.

sketch0.title: A Commit Is a Deliberate Checkpoint
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
A commit is a named checkpoint in the repository timeline. It records the staged snapshot, author identity, message, and link to the previous commit.

A useful checkpoint answers one question: what meaningful change became true here? It might add opening hours, correct a contact address, or remove an obsolete page.

Avoid treating a commit as a periodic save of everything in sight. Review the staged snapshot first.

sketch1.title: Commit Only the Staged Snapshot
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
After reviewing `git diff --staged`, record the snapshot:

```bash
git commit -m "Add evening workshop schedule"
```

Git commits only the staging area. Unstaged edits remain in the working tree, ready for later work.

That boundary is what allows two changes made on the same afternoon to become two understandable commits.

sketch2.title: Write the Message for a Reader
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
A commit subject should be brief, specific, and written as an action: `Add evening workshop schedule`, not `changes` or `worked on page`.

Imagine the message completing the sentence “This commit will …”. The wording should describe the result, not the commands you typed.

Good messages turn `git log --oneline` into a useful project summary.

sketch3.title: Verify the Result
sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
After committing, read both history and current state:

```bash
git log --oneline -1
git status --short
```

The first command confirms the newest message. The second reveals whether anything remains unstaged or untracked.

Verification closes the loop: you know what entered history and what still needs attention.
